hook.title: Physical symptoms I didn't realize were actually caused by anxiety
hook.subtitle: (and how my therapist taught me to deal with them.)

slides.title: Waking up with immediate dread.
slides.body: The Lie: "I just hate mornings."
slides.reality: The Reality: My cortisol was spiking the second I opened my eyes. My body was reacting to the day like it was a tiger.

slides.title: Staying in bed for hours on my phone.
slides.body: The Lie: "I'm just lazy and slow."
slides.reality: The Reality: It was "functional freeze." I was too overwhelmed to start, so I scrolled to numb the panic.

slides.title: Feeling nauseous before breakfast.
slides.body: The Lie: "I'm not a breakfast person."
slides.reality: The Reality: Anxiety diverts blood away from your stomach. I literally couldn't digest food because my body sensed danger.

slides.title: Rushing but getting nowhere.
slides.body: The Lie: "I have poor time management."
slides.reality: The Reality: My brain was moving so fast it couldn't focus on one task. I was running on adrenaline, not focus.

slides.title: What actually helped
slides.body: I stopped checking emails from bed. That was just fueling the fire. Now, I take two minutes to just open Nafsy and tap out how I'm feeling.
slides.reality: Getting the dread out of my head and onto a screen makes it feel manageable, not monstrous.

slides.title: Scrolling for 3 hours without enjoying it.
slides.body: The Lie: "I'm just decompressing."
slides.reality: The Reality: I wanted to get up, but I physically couldn't. My nervous system slammed on the brakes to protect me.

slides.title: Procrastinating tiny tasks (like showering).
slides.body: The Lie: "I'll do it in 5 minutes."
slides.reality: The Reality: The transition felt unsafe. Staying still felt like the only safe option.

slides.title: Brain fog / Zoning out.
slides.body: The Lie: "I'm just tired."
slides.reality: The Reality: My brain hit "shutdown" mode because the internal pressure was too high.

slides.title: Shame spiraling while sitting still.
slides.body: The Lie: "I am wasting my life."
slides.reality: The Reality: You aren't wasting life; you are surviving a nervous system crash.

slides.title: How I melt the ice
slides.body: Forcing myself to move never worked. It just added shame. I started just admitting it. I log "I feel stuck" into Nafsy.
slides.reality: Weirdly, just naming the freeze without judging it usually gives me just enough energy to put the phone down.

slides.title: Analyzing their response time.
slides.body: The Lie: "I just want to know when we're hanging out."
slides.reality: The Reality: A delayed text felt like abandonment. I was counting minutes to prove I was safe.

slides.title: Creating problems to fix.
slides.body: The Lie: "I just want us to communicate better."
slides.reality: The Reality: I was picking fights just to get a reaction. Any attention felt better than silence.

slides.title: Shape-shifting into what they like.
slides.body: The Lie: "We have so much in common!"
slides.reality: The Reality: I was suppressing my real self because I didn't think the "real me" was lovable.

slides.title: Over-functioning.
slides.body: The Lie: "I love doing things for people."
slides.reality: The Reality: I was trying to make myself indispensable so they couldn't leave me.

slides.title: Breaking the pattern
slides.body: I realized I was looking for them to soothe me, but that's an inside job. Now, when I feel the urge to double-text or spiral, I vent it into Nafsy first.
slides.reality: Usually, once I read it back, I realize I don't need to send it. I just needed to feel safe.

slides.title: Weekend Migraines.
slides.body: The Lie: "I must be dehydrated."
slides.reality: The Reality: My stress hormones dropped too fast on Friday night, causing my blood vessels to rapidly expand (pain).

slides.title: Feeling bored/uneasy when it's quiet.
slides.body: The Lie: "I need to be productive."
slides.reality: The Reality: My body is addicted to stress chemicals. Calmness actually feels dangerous to me.

slides.title: Sunday Scaries starting on Saturday.
slides.body: The Lie: "I hate my job."
slides.reality: The Reality: It's anticipatory anxiety. I'm already bracing for the next hit of stress.

slides.title: Getting a cold the moment I take a break.
slides.body: The Lie: "Bad timing."
slides.reality: The Reality: Stress suppresses the immune system. When I finally stopped, my immune system crashed.

slides.title: What actually helped
slides.body: I had to teach my body that "safe" doesn't mean "boring." I use Nafsy to track my mood on weekends specifically.
slides.reality: It reminds me that I'm allowed to just exist without producing anything. It's a slow process, but it helps.

slides.title: Snapping at people I love.
slides.body: The Lie: "They are being so annoying."
slides.reality: The Reality: I was at capacity. They weren't annoying; I just had zero patience left to give.

slides.title: Rage cleaning.
slides.body: The Lie: "No one helps me around here."
slides.reality: The Reality: The clutter made me feel out of control. Cleaning was a frantic attempt to regain order.

slides.title: Road rage / Impatience.
slides.body: The Lie: "Everyone is an idiot."
slides.reality: The Reality: Being stuck in traffic made me feel trapped. It was a fight-or-flight response to restriction.

slides.title: Sensory sensitivity.
slides.body: The Lie: "Turn that TV down!"
slides.reality: The Reality: My brain was over-processing sound. The noise wasn't loud; my nerves were raw.

slides.title: The change
slides.body: I realized my anger was just a bodyguard for my anxiety. Instead of exploding, I started catching the "overwhelm" earlier.
slides.reality: I use Nafsy to vent the rage privately. It's better to scream into an app than at my family.

slides.title: Reading reviews for 45 minutes.
slides.body: The Lie: "I want to find the best place."
slides.reality: The Reality: I was terrified of making a "bad" choice and regretting it.

slides.title: Asking 5 people for their opinion.
slides.body: The Lie: "I value their input."
slides.reality: The Reality: I didn't trust myself. I wanted someone else to take the blame if it went wrong.

slides.title: "I don't care, you pick."
slides.body: The Lie: "I'm easygoing."
slides.reality: The Reality: I was so mentally drained that making one more choice felt physically painful.

slides.title: Post-decision panic.
slides.body: The Lie: "I should have picked the other one."
slides.reality: The Reality: Perfectionism. I believed there was a "perfect" choice that would save me from disappointment.

slides.title: How I started trusting myself
slides.body: I realized the stakes were actually really low. It's just dinner. Now, when I feel the paralysis setting in, I just note it in Nafsy: "I'm scared of getting this wrong."
slides.reality: Acknowledging the fear usually shrinks it enough for me to just... pick something.
